title: Processing Rules for Data Validation

Processing Rules for Data Validation

Processing Rules for Data Validation

Uniface data validation occurs in a specific sequence that is determined by the active path, the type of validation, and the way validation was started.

Premises

The validation rules are based on the following premises:

  • All modified data is subject to validation, whether modified by the user or by ProcScript.
  • Declarative validation occurs before procedural validation for a given field or entity. Uniface checks the field syntax definition, the syntax as determined for the data type, and the current ValRep defined for each field for which data validation has to be carried out.
  • Procedural validation is provided by the data validation triggers:
    • validate (Field)
    • validateKey
    • validate (Entity)

    Uniface activates these triggers as needed, when carrying out data validation checks.

  • The trigger sequence activated when the active path changes always follows the rule that data validation triggers are fired before any other trigger.

    Uniface activates the field-level validate trigger first, if it has not been already activated.

    If there are outstanding validateKey and validate trigger checks, the validateKey is activated before any of the outstanding validate triggers.

    In forms, the corresponding loseFocus or leaveModified trigger is activated just after each validate trigger.

  • You can use the functions $fieldcheck, $keycheck, or $occcheck to explicitly force validation to occur when it would otherwise not occur.
  • For form components only:
    • There is no direct mapping between a structure editor function (for example ^STORE) and the trigger with the same name (store). When ^STORE reaches the beginning of the structure editor’s input queue, the structure editor activates the store trigger, after any outstanding data validation checks on the active path have been successfully carried out.
    • Structure editor functions are input to the structure editor and not the actual functions of the structure editor; they can be seen as requests for a certain kind of processing than actual functions.
    • You cannot redefine the processing that the structure editor carries out once a request is received. This means that, although ProcScript in triggers can cause different things to happen, the processing that the structure editor comes out when it receives a request, is fixed.

Rules

The rules of data validation are as follows:

  1. If the active path changes, Uniface carries out all outstanding data validation for the changed part of the active path that is, for the part of the path which has changed but is no longer active.

    If the path changes from a modified occurrence of an outer entity to an occurrence of an inner entity, an entity is added to the active path while the occurrence of the outer entity is still in the active path. This type of path change, therefore, cannot activate the validate trigger of the outer entity.

  2. In form components only:
    • The component-level structure editor functions ^CLEAR, ^ERASE, ^QUIT, and ^RETRIEVE instantly drop all outstanding data validation checks without carrying them out.
    • The component-level structure editor functions ^ACCEPT and ^STORE immediately cause the structure editor to carry out all outstanding data validation on the active path. This occurs until either a successful completion of the function (activation of the accept or store trigger), or an error occurs. The process stops at the point where the error occurs if an error is encountered.
  3. A field, key, or occurrence is validated if one of these cases holds:
    • It has been modified, but not yet validated
    • It has not been modified, but validation is demanded by the corresponding checking function ($fieldcheck, $keycheck, or $occcheck).
  4. The data validation process operates in a depth-first fashion:
    • Before an occurrence can be validated, all its keys and fields must be validated.
    • Before a key can be validated, all its fields must be validated.

Predicting Application Behavior

You can always predict the behavior of your application if you use a combination of the high-level information provided here and the low-level information provided in the descriptions of triggers and statements. For more information, see Uniface Reference and Data Validation.

If you encounter any problems, put the component or application into debug mode and use the Uniface Debugger. This sends trigger processing sequence information to the message frame. You can access the message frame while in debug mode.
